cards.definition: the control centre of the cell — contains DNA and controls all the cell's activities
a thin, flexible layer surrounding all cells — controls what enters and leaves
a jelly-like fluid filling the cell — where most chemical reactions take place
organelles where aerobic respiration takes place — produce energy (ATP) for the cell
tiny organelles where proteins are made (synthesised)
a rigid layer outside the cell membrane in plant cells — made of cellulose, provides support
organelle found in plant cells — contains chlorophyll and is the site of photosynthesis
a large, fluid-filled sac in plant cells — stores cell sap and helps maintain the cell's shape
a small, circular loop of DNA found in bacterial cells — separate from the main chromosome
a tail-like structure on some bacterial cells — used for movement

questions.question: What is the function of the nucleus?
options: B) Controls the cell and contains DNA
D) Controls what enters the cell
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre — it contains DNA which directs all cell activities.

questions.question: Which organelle is the site of aerobic respiration?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration, releasing energy (ATP) the cell can use.

questions.question: What is the function of ribosomes?
questions.explanation: Ribosomes are the site of protein synthesis — they build proteins from amino acids using instructions from DNA.

questions.question: Which structure is found in plant cells but NOT animal cells?
questions.explanation: Plant cells have a cell wall made of cellulose. Animal cells do not have a cell wall.

questions.question: What does the cell membrane do?
options: B) Controls what enters and leaves the cell
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is a selectively permeable barrier controlling which substances move in and out of the cell.

questions.question: Where does photosynthesis take place in a plant cell?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis in plant cells.

questions.question: What is cytoplasm?
options: B) A jelly-like fluid where reactions occur
C) A structure that makes energy
D) A circular loop of DNA
questions.explanation: Cytoplasm is the jelly-like fluid that fills the cell. Most chemical reactions in the cell occur in the cytoplasm.

questions.question: What is the function of the vacuole in plant cells?
options: C) Stores cell sap and maintains shape
questions.explanation: The large vacuole in plant cells is filled with cell sap. It helps maintain the shape and firmness of the cell.

questions.question: What is a plasmid?
options: A) A large organelle in plant cells
B) A small circular loop of DNA in bacteria
C) The control centre of an animal cell
questions.explanation: Plasmids are small, circular loops of DNA found in bacterial cells, separate from the main chromosome.

questions.question: What is the function of a flagellum?
questions.explanation: A flagellum is a tail-like structure used by some bacterial cells to move through their environment.

questions.question: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell.
questions.explanation: The nucleus contains DNA which controls all cell activities, making it the cell's control centre.

questions.question: Mitochondria are found in plant cells but not animal cells.
questions.explanation: Mitochondria are found in both plant and animal cells. They are the site of aerobic respiration in all eukaryotic cells.

questions.question: Ribosomes are where proteins are synthesised.
questions.explanation: Ribosomes read the instructions in mRNA and assemble amino acids into proteins.

questions.question: The cell wall in plants is made of cellulose.
questions.explanation: Plant cell walls are made of cellulose, a strong carbohydrate that provides structural support.

questions.question: The cell membrane is found only in animal cells.
questions.explanation: The cell membrane surrounds all cells — both plant and animal. Plant cells have a cell wall outside the membrane.

questions.question: Chloroplasts are the site of photosynthesis.
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll which absorbs light energy to drive photosynthesis.

questions.question: The vacuole in plant cells is small and found near the nucleus.
questions.explanation: Plant cells have a large, permanent central vacuole filled with cell sap that helps maintain the cell's shape.

questions.question: Bacterial cells contain a nucleus.
questions.explanation: Bacteria are prokaryotes — they have no true nucleus. Their DNA floats freely in the cytoplasm.

questions.question: Plasmids are circular loops of DNA found in bacteria.
questions.explanation: Plasmids are small, circular DNA molecules in bacteria, separate from the main chromosome.

questions.question: A flagellum helps a bacterial cell to move.
questions.explanation: The flagellum is a rotating tail-like structure that propels the bacterium through its environment.

questions.question: The ________ is the control centre of the cell and contains DNA.
questions.explanation: The nucleus contains chromosomes (DNA) and controls all cell activities.

questions.question: The ________ is where aerobic respiration takes place in a cell.
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration, producing ATP energy for the cell.

questions.question: ________ are the organelles where proteins are synthesised.
questions.explanation: Ribosomes translate mRNA into proteins using amino acids.

questions.question: Plant cells have a ________ made of cellulose outside the cell membrane.
questions.explanation: The cell wall provides support and structure to plant cells.

questions.question: The cell ________ controls what enters and leaves the cell.
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is a selectively permeable barrier regulating the movement of substances.

questions.question: ________ contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis.
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts are found in plant cells and are where photosynthesis occurs.

questions.question: The large ________ in plant cells stores cell sap and helps maintain cell shape.
questions.explanation: The central vacuole is a key feature of mature plant cells.

questions.question: Bacterial DNA floats freely in the ________ as there is no nucleus.
questions.explanation: Bacteria are prokaryotes — they have no membrane-bound nucleus, so DNA is in the cytoplasm.

questions.question: A ________ is a small circular loop of DNA found in bacterial cells.
questions.explanation: Plasmids are separate from the main bacterial chromosome and often carry useful genes.

questions.question: A ________ is a tail-like structure that helps bacteria move.
questions.explanation: The flagellum rotates to propel the bacterium through liquid environments.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: control centre — contains DNA
site of aerobic respiration
site of protein synthesis
site of photosynthesis — contains chlorophyll
rigid cellulose layer — supports plant cells

cards.definition: cell division that produces two genetically identical daughter cells — used for growth and repair
cell division that produces four genetically unique cells with half the chromosome number — makes gametes
a thread-like structure of DNA carrying genetic information — humans have 46 in 23 pairs
the process by which a cell becomes specialised for a particular function
an undifferentiated cell that can divide and develop into different specialised cell types
a stem cell from an early embryo — can differentiate into any cell type in the body
a stem cell found in specific tissues — can only differentiate into a limited range of cell types
uncontrolled cell division caused by mutations in genes that control the cell cycle
a sex cell — sperm (male) or egg (female) — contains half the normal chromosome number
the fusion of a male gamete (sperm) and female gamete (egg) to form a zygote

questions.question: What type of cell division produces two genetically identical cells?
questions.explanation: Mitosis produces two daughter cells that are genetically identical to the parent cell — used for growth and repair.

questions.question: How many cells does meiosis produce?
questions.explanation: Meiosis produces four genetically unique daughter cells, each with half the normal chromosome number.

questions.question: How many chromosomes do human body cells normally contain?
questions.explanation: Human body cells contain 46 chromosomes arranged in 23 pairs. Gametes contain only 23 (one from each pair).

questions.question: What is differentiation?
options: A) Cell division producing identical cells
B) A cell becoming specialised for a specific function
C) The fusion of two gametes
questions.explanation: Differentiation is the process by which an unspecialised cell develops into a specialised cell with a specific structure and function.

questions.question: Which type of stem cell can differentiate into ANY cell type in the body?
questions.explanation: Embryonic stem cells are pluripotent — they can differentiate into any type of cell in the body.

questions.question: What is cancer?
questions.explanation: Cancer occurs when mutations in genes that control cell division cause cells to divide uncontrollably.

questions.question: What is a gamete?
options: A) A body cell with 46 chromosomes
B) A sex cell with half the normal chromosome number
C) A stem cell that can become any cell type
D) A cell produced by mitosis
questions.explanation: Gametes (sperm and egg cells) are sex cells produced by meiosis. They contain 23 chromosomes — half the normal number.

questions.question: What happens during fertilisation?
options: C) A sperm and egg cell fuse
questions.explanation: Fertilisation is the fusion of a sperm cell and an egg cell to form a zygote with the full 46 chromosomes.

questions.question: Where are adult stem cells found?
options: B) In specific tissues of the body
D) They do not exist in adults
questions.explanation: Adult stem cells are found in specific tissues such as bone marrow and can differentiate into a limited range of cell types.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of mitosis in a multicellular organism?
options: C) Growth and repair of tissues
questions.explanation: Mitosis is used for growth (increasing cell number) and repair (replacing damaged or dead cells), producing identical copies.

questions.question: Mitosis produces two genetically identical daughter cells.
questions.explanation: Mitosis produces two cells with the same genetic information as the parent cell — used for growth and repair.

questions.question: Meiosis is used for growth and repair of body tissues.
questions.explanation: Meiosis is used to produce gametes (sex cells). Mitosis is used for growth and repair.

questions.question: Human body cells contain 46 chromosomes.
questions.explanation: Human body cells are diploid — they contain 46 chromosomes in 23 pairs.

questions.question: All cells in the body remain unspecialised throughout life.
questions.explanation: During development, most cells undergo differentiation to become specialised for specific functions.

questions.question: Embryonic stem cells can become any type of cell in the body.
questions.explanation: Embryonic stem cells are pluripotent — they can differentiate into any cell type.

questions.question: Cancer is caused by controlled cell division.
questions.explanation: Cancer is caused by uncontrolled cell division — mutations in genes that regulate the cell cycle lead to tumour formation.

questions.question: Gametes contain the full 46 chromosomes.
questions.explanation: Gametes are haploid — they contain only 23 chromosomes (half the normal number) so that fertilisation restores 46.

questions.question: Fertilisation produces a cell called a zygote.
questions.explanation: When a sperm fertilises an egg, the resulting cell is called a zygote — it has the full 46 chromosomes.

questions.question: Adult stem cells can differentiate into any type of cell.
questions.explanation: Adult stem cells are more limited than embryonic stem cells — they can only differentiate into a restricted range of cell types.

questions.question: Meiosis produces cells that are genetically different from each other.
questions.explanation: Meiosis introduces genetic variation through crossing over and independent assortment, producing four unique cells.

questions.question: ________ produces two genetically identical daughter cells and is used for growth and repair.
questions.explanation: Mitosis is the type of cell division that produces identical copies for growth and tissue repair.

questions.question: ________ produces four genetically unique cells with half the chromosome number.
questions.explanation: Meiosis is the type of cell division that produces gametes — it halves the chromosome number.

questions.question: Human body cells contain ________ chromosomes arranged in 23 pairs.
questions.explanation: Body cells are diploid (2n = 46). Gametes are haploid (n = 23).

questions.question: The process by which a cell becomes specialised is called ________.
questions.explanation: During differentiation, cells develop specific structures and functions.

questions.question: A ________ cell is undifferentiated and can divide to produce specialised cells.
questions.explanation: Stem cells are unspecialised cells with the ability to self-renew and differentiate.

questions.question: ________ stem cells from early embryos can differentiate into any cell type.
questions.explanation: Embryonic stem cells are pluripotent — they can become any of the ~200 cell types in the human body.

questions.question: Uncontrolled cell division caused by gene mutations is called ________.
questions.explanation: Cancer occurs when the normal regulation of the cell cycle breaks down, causing cells to divide without limit.

questions.question: Sex cells such as sperm and eggs are called ________.
questions.explanation: Gametes are produced by meiosis and contain half the normal chromosome number.

questions.question: The fusion of a sperm and egg cell during ________ restores the full chromosome number.
questions.explanation: Fertilisation combines the 23 chromosomes from each gamete to produce a zygote with 46 chromosomes.

questions.question: ________ stem cells found in tissues can only differentiate into a limited range of cell types.
questions.explanation: Adult stem cells are multipotent — more restricted than embryonic stem cells.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: produces two genetically identical daughter cells
produces four unique cells with half the chromosome number
process of a cell becoming specialised
undifferentiated cell that can become specialised
sex cell containing half the normal chromosome number

cards.definition: the organ system that breaks down food into small molecules that can be absorbed into the blood
a biological catalyst that speeds up chemical reactions in living organisms without being used up
an enzyme that breaks down starch into maltose — produced in the salivary glands and pancreas
an enzyme that breaks down proteins into amino acids — produced in the stomach and pancreas
an enzyme that breaks down lipids (fats) into fatty acids and glycerol — produced in the pancreas
a substance produced by the liver and stored in the gall bladder — neutralises stomach acid and emulsifies fats
finger-like projections lining the small intestine — increase surface area for absorption of nutrients
the wave-like muscular contractions that push food along the digestive tract
the organ that absorbs water from undigested food — remaining material forms faeces
a muscular organ producing hydrochloric acid and protease — breaks down food by physical and chemical digestion

questions.question: What is the function of the digestive system?
options: A) To pump blood around the body
B) To break down food for absorption into the blood
D) To filter waste from the blood
questions.explanation: The digestive system breaks food down into small soluble molecules that can be absorbed into the bloodstream.

questions.question: What type of molecule is an enzyme?
questions.explanation: Enzymes are proteins with a specific shape. Their active site fits one type of substrate — like a lock and key.

questions.question: Which enzyme breaks down starch?
questions.explanation: Amylase breaks starch down into maltose. It is produced in the salivary glands and the pancreas.

questions.question: What does protease break down?
questions.explanation: Protease enzymes break proteins down into amino acids. They are produced in the stomach and pancreas.

questions.question: What is the product of lipase digestion?
questions.explanation: Lipase breaks down lipids (fats and oils) into fatty acids and glycerol.

questions.question: What is the role of bile in digestion?
options: C) It neutralises acid and emulsifies fats
questions.explanation: Bile is made in the liver and stored in the gall bladder. It neutralises stomach acid and emulsifies fats into droplets, increasing surface area for lipase.

questions.question: What is the function of villi in the small intestine?
options: B) To increase surface area for absorption
questions.explanation: Villi are finger-like projections that massively increase the surface area of the small intestine for efficient nutrient absorption.

questions.question: What is peristalsis?
options: A) The breakdown of food by enzymes
B) The absorption of nutrients into the blood
C) Wave-like muscle contractions moving food along the gut
D) The production of stomach acid
questions.explanation: Peristalsis is the coordinated muscular contractions of the gut wall that push food along the digestive tract.

questions.question: Where is water mainly absorbed in the digestive system?
questions.explanation: The large intestine absorbs water from undigested food material. What remains becomes faeces.

questions.question: Which acid does the stomach produce?
questions.explanation: The stomach produces hydrochloric acid (HCl) to kill bacteria in food and provide the acidic pH needed for stomach protease to work.

questions.question: Enzymes are biological catalysts made of protein.
questions.explanation: Enzymes are proteins with a specific 3D shape. They speed up chemical reactions without being used up themselves.

questions.question: Amylase breaks down proteins into amino acids.
questions.explanation: Amylase breaks down starch into maltose. Protease is the enzyme that breaks down proteins.

questions.question: Lipase is produced in the pancreas.
questions.explanation: Lipase is one of several enzymes produced by the pancreas. It breaks down lipids into fatty acids and glycerol.

questions.question: Bile is produced in the gall bladder.
questions.explanation: Bile is produced in the liver and stored in the gall bladder before being released into the small intestine.

questions.question: Villi increase the surface area of the small intestine.
questions.explanation: The finger-like villi greatly increase the surface area of the small intestine, allowing efficient absorption of digested nutrients.

questions.question: Peristalsis is a chemical process that breaks down food.
questions.explanation: Peristalsis is a physical process — wave-like muscle contractions in the gut wall that push food along the digestive tract.

questions.question: The large intestine is where most nutrient absorption occurs.
questions.explanation: Most nutrient absorption occurs in the small intestine. The large intestine mainly absorbs water.

questions.question: The stomach produces hydrochloric acid.
questions.explanation: The stomach produces HCl to kill microbes in food and to provide the acidic conditions needed for protease enzymes to work.

questions.question: Protease breaks down lipids into fatty acids and glycerol.
questions.explanation: Protease breaks down proteins into amino acids. Lipase breaks down lipids into fatty acids and glycerol.

questions.question: Digestive enzymes are not used up during reactions.
questions.explanation: As biological catalysts, enzymes speed up reactions but are not consumed. They can be reused many times.

questions.question: An ________ is a biological catalyst that speeds up chemical reactions without being used up.
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower the activation energy of reactions in living organisms.

questions.question: ________ is an enzyme that breaks down starch into maltose.
questions.explanation: Amylase is produced in the salivary glands and pancreas and digests starch.

questions.question: ________ breaks down proteins into amino acids.
questions.explanation: Protease enzymes are produced in the stomach and pancreas to digest dietary protein.

questions.question: ________ breaks down lipids into fatty acids and glycerol.
questions.explanation: Lipase is produced in the pancreas and acts in the small intestine to digest fats.

questions.question: ________ is produced by the liver, stored in the gall bladder, and emulsifies fats.
questions.explanation: Bile neutralises stomach acid and breaks fats into smaller droplets (emulsification) to increase surface area for lipase.

questions.question: ________ are finger-like projections in the small intestine that increase surface area for absorption.
questions.explanation: Each villus has microvilli (brush border) and a good blood supply to maximise nutrient absorption.

questions.question: ________ are wave-like muscle contractions that push food through the digestive tract.
questions.explanation: Peristalsis involves circular and longitudinal muscles in the gut wall working alternately.

questions.question: Water is mainly absorbed in the ________ intestine.
questions.explanation: The large intestine absorbs water from indigestible food material before it is excreted as faeces.

questions.question: The ________ produces hydrochloric acid and protease to begin protein digestion.
questions.explanation: The stomach's acidic environment (pH 2) kills bacteria and activates pepsinogen to form the protease pepsin.

questions.question: The digestive system breaks food into small molecules that are absorbed into the ________.
questions.explanation: Digested nutrients pass through the wall of the small intestine into the bloodstream for transport around the body.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: breaks down starch into maltose
breaks down proteins into amino acids
breaks down lipids into fatty acids and glycerol
emulsifies fats and neutralises stomach acid
increase surface area for absorption in the small intestine

cards.definition: a microorganism that causes disease — includes bacteria, viruses, fungi, and protists
single-celled prokaryotic microorganisms — some cause disease by releasing toxins
a non-living particle that reproduces inside host cells — can cause disease by damaging cells
a protein on the surface of a pathogen that triggers an immune response
a protein produced by lymphocytes (white blood cells) that binds to and destroys a specific antigen
the process by which white blood cells (phagocytes) engulf and digest pathogens
a type of white blood cell that produces antibodies to fight specific pathogens
introducing a harmless form of a pathogen into the body to stimulate antibody production without causing disease
when enough of the population is immune to a disease that it cannot easily spread — protecting those who cannot be vaccinated
a medicine that kills or stops the growth of bacteria — does not work against viruses

questions.question: What is a pathogen?
options: A) A type of white blood cell
B) A microorganism that causes disease
C) A protein that fights infection
D) A medicine that kills bacteria
questions.explanation: Pathogens are disease-causing microorganisms including bacteria, viruses, fungi, and protists.

questions.question: How do bacteria cause disease?
options: A) By invading and destroying cell nuclei
B) By reproducing inside host cells only
questions.explanation: Bacteria can cause illness by releasing toxins (poisons) that damage the body's cells and tissues.

questions.question: How do viruses cause disease?
options: B) By reproducing inside and damaging host cells
D) By infecting only dead cells
questions.explanation: Viruses reproduce by hijacking host cells. This damages or destroys the cells, causing symptoms of disease.

questions.question: What triggers an immune response?
questions.explanation: Antigens are proteins on the surface of pathogens. The immune system recognises them as foreign and mounts a response.

questions.question: What do lymphocytes produce to fight infection?
questions.explanation: Lymphocytes produce antibodies — proteins that are complementary to specific antigens and destroy pathogens.

questions.question: What is phagocytosis?
options: B) White blood cells engulfing and digesting pathogens
D) A virus reproducing inside a cell
questions.explanation: Phagocytosis is the process by which phagocytes (a type of white blood cell) ingest and destroy pathogens.

questions.question: How does vaccination protect against disease?
options: A) It kills all bacteria in the body
B) It introduces a live pathogen to cause disease
C) It stimulates antibody production without causing disease
D) It replaces white blood cells
questions.explanation: Vaccines contain a harmless or dead form of a pathogen. They stimulate the immune system to produce antibodies so the body is ready if exposed to the real pathogen.

questions.question: What is herd immunity?
options: B) When enough people are immune that disease cannot spread easily
C) A type of antibiotic treatment
D) The body's first line of defence
questions.explanation: Herd immunity is achieved when a high enough proportion of the population is immune, making it hard for disease to spread and protecting those who cannot be vaccinated.

questions.question: Against which type of pathogen do antibiotics work?
questions.explanation: Antibiotics kill bacteria or prevent them from reproducing. They have no effect on viruses — antibiotics should not be prescribed for viral infections.

questions.question: What type of white blood cell engulfs pathogens?
questions.explanation: Phagocytes are white blood cells that engulf and destroy pathogens through phagocytosis.

questions.question: A pathogen is any microorganism that causes disease.
questions.explanation: Pathogens include bacteria, viruses, fungi, and protists — all microorganisms capable of causing disease.

questions.question: Bacteria cause disease by reproducing inside host cells.
questions.explanation: Bacteria cause disease mainly by releasing toxins. It is viruses that reproduce inside host cells.

questions.question: Antigens are proteins found on the surface of pathogens.
questions.explanation: Antigens are surface proteins on pathogens that the immune system recognises as foreign.

questions.question: Antibodies are produced by phagocytes.
questions.explanation: Antibodies are produced by lymphocytes (a different type of white blood cell). Phagocytes engulf pathogens.

questions.question: Each antibody is specific to one type of antigen.
questions.explanation: Antibodies have a complementary shape to one specific antigen — this is the basis of specific immunity.

questions.question: Vaccination introduces a live, active pathogen into the body.
questions.explanation: Vaccines contain dead, weakened, or harmless forms of pathogens — they stimulate immunity without causing disease.

questions.question: Antibiotics can be used to treat viral infections like the flu.
questions.explanation: Antibiotics only work against bacteria. They have no effect on viruses — using them for viral infections is ineffective and promotes antibiotic resistance.

questions.question: Phagocytes engulf and digest pathogens.
questions.explanation: Phagocytosis involves phagocytes surrounding a pathogen, engulfing it, and using enzymes to break it down.

questions.question: Herd immunity can protect people who are unable to be vaccinated.
questions.explanation: When enough people are immune, disease cannot spread easily, protecting vulnerable individuals who cannot receive vaccines.

questions.question: Memory cells allow a faster immune response on re-exposure to a pathogen.
questions.explanation: After an infection or vaccination, memory cells remain in the blood. On re-exposure, they rapidly produce large quantities of antibodies.

questions.question: A ________ is a microorganism that causes disease.
questions.explanation: Pathogens include bacteria, viruses, fungi, and protists.

questions.question: Bacteria cause disease mainly by releasing ________.
questions.explanation: Bacterial toxins poison body cells and cause the symptoms of disease.

questions.question: Viruses cause disease by reproducing inside ________ cells.
questions.explanation: Viruses hijack host cell machinery to replicate, damaging or destroying the host cells in the process.

questions.question: ________ are proteins on the surface of pathogens that trigger the immune response.
questions.explanation: Antigens are recognised as foreign by the immune system, triggering production of specific antibodies.

questions.question: ________ produced by lymphocytes bind to specific antigens and destroy pathogens.
questions.explanation: Antibodies have a complementary shape to one antigen — they are highly specific.

questions.question: ________ is the process by which white blood cells engulf and digest pathogens.
questions.explanation: Phagocytes ingest pathogens and use enzymes to break them down.

questions.question: ________ introduces a harmless form of a pathogen to stimulate the immune system.
questions.explanation: Vaccines cause the immune system to produce antibodies and memory cells without causing the disease.

questions.question: ________ immunity occurs when enough of a population is immune to prevent disease spreading.
questions.explanation: Herd immunity protects vulnerable people who cannot be vaccinated.

questions.question: ________ kill bacteria but do not work against viruses.
questions.explanation: Antibiotics target bacterial cell structures. Since viruses use host cells, antibiotics have no effect on them.

questions.question: White blood cells called ________ engulf and digest pathogens.
questions.explanation: Phagocytes are the first responders of the immune system, ingesting pathogens non-specifically.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: microorganism that causes disease
protein on pathogen surface that triggers immune response
protein produced by lymphocytes that destroys a specific pathogen
white blood cells engulfing and digesting pathogens
stimulating immunity without causing disease

cards.definition: the process by which plants use light energy to convert carbon dioxide and water into glucose and oxygen
the green pigment in chloroplasts that absorbs light energy for photosynthesis
a factor that is in short supply and prevents photosynthesis from occurring faster
the brightness of light — a key limiting factor for photosynthesis
the amount of CO₂ in the air — a limiting factor for photosynthesis at low concentrations
affects the rate of enzyme-controlled reactions in photosynthesis — too high denatures enzymes
the sugar produced by photosynthesis — used for respiration, stored as starch, or used to make cellulose and proteins
the insoluble storage form of glucose in plants — converted back to glucose when needed
light intensity is inversely proportional to the square of the distance from the light source
the light intensity at which the rate of photosynthesis equals the rate of respiration — no net gas exchange

questions.question: What are the products of photosynthesis?
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis converts CO₂ and water into glucose (for energy/growth) and oxygen (released as a by-product).

questions.question: What does chlorophyll do?
options: B) Absorbs light energy for photosynthesis
D) Absorbs water from the soil
questions.explanation: Chlorophyll is the green pigment in chloroplasts that absorbs light energy (mainly red and blue wavelengths) to drive photosynthesis.

questions.question: Which of these is a limiting factor for photosynthesis?
questions.explanation: Light intensity is one of three main limiting factors for photosynthesis — along with CO₂ concentration and temperature.

questions.question: What happens to glucose produced in photosynthesis?
options: A) It is all immediately released into the air
B) It is only used for respiration
C) It can be used for respiration, stored as starch, or used to make cellulose and proteins
D) It is converted to carbon dioxide
questions.explanation: Plants use glucose for respiration (energy), store it as starch, or use it to build cellulose (cell walls) and amino acids (proteins).

questions.question: Why does increasing temperature beyond the optimum reduce photosynthesis?
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis involves enzyme-controlled reactions. Temperatures above the optimum change the shape of enzyme active sites (denaturation), reducing the rate.

questions.question: What is a limiting factor?
options: A) A factor that speeds up photosynthesis
B) A factor in short supply that prevents a faster rate
D) An enzyme used in photosynthesis
questions.explanation: A limiting factor is the factor that is in shortest supply at any given time — it prevents photosynthesis from going faster even if other conditions are improved.

questions.question: In which form do plants store glucose?
questions.explanation: Plants convert glucose to starch for storage because starch is insoluble and does not affect osmosis. It can be converted back to glucose when needed.

questions.question: What is the word equation for photosynthesis?
options: A) Glucose + Oxygen → Carbon dioxide + Water
B) Carbon dioxide + Water → Glucose + Oxygen
D) Carbon dioxide + Oxygen → Glucose + Water
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: Carbon dioxide + Water → Glucose + Oxygen. Light energy is needed and the process occurs in chloroplasts.

questions.question: What happens at the compensation point?
options: C) Rate of photosynthesis equals rate of respiration
D) All glucose is converted to starch
questions.explanation: At the compensation point, the rate of photosynthesis exactly matches the rate of respiration — there is no net uptake or release of CO₂ or O₂.

questions.question: How does increasing CO₂ concentration affect photosynthesis?
options: C) It increases the rate until another factor becomes limiting
questions.explanation: Increasing CO₂ concentration increases the rate of photosynthesis until another factor (e.g. light intensity) becomes the limiting factor.

questions.question: Photosynthesis produces glucose and oxygen.
questions.explanation: The products of photosynthesis are glucose (for energy and growth) and oxygen (released as a by-product).

questions.question: Chlorophyll is found in the mitochondria of plant cells.
questions.explanation: Chlorophyll is found in the chloroplasts, not the mitochondria. Chloroplasts are the organelles where photosynthesis occurs.

questions.question: Light intensity is a limiting factor for photosynthesis.
questions.explanation: If light intensity is low, it limits the rate of photosynthesis even if CO₂ and temperature are at optimum levels.

questions.question: Plants store glucose as glycogen.
questions.explanation: Plants store glucose as starch. Glycogen is the storage form of glucose in animals and fungi.

questions.question: Increasing temperature always increases the rate of photosynthesis.
questions.explanation: Increasing temperature increases the rate up to the optimum. Above the optimum, enzymes are denatured and the rate falls.

questions.question: Carbon dioxide is a raw material (reactant) in photosynthesis.
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis requires CO₂ and water as reactants, using light energy in the chloroplasts.

questions.question: The compensation point is where photosynthesis equals respiration.
questions.explanation: At the compensation point, all the oxygen produced by photosynthesis is used in respiration and vice versa — no net gas exchange.

questions.question: Photosynthesis takes place in the chloroplasts.
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and all the enzymes needed for photosynthesis.

questions.question: Glucose produced by photosynthesis can be used to make cellulose.
questions.explanation: Plants use glucose to synthesise cellulose (for cell walls), amino acids (for proteins), and starch (for storage).

questions.question: Oxygen is a limiting factor for photosynthesis.
questions.explanation: Oxygen is a product, not a reactant. The three main limiting factors are light intensity, CO₂ concentration, and temperature.

questions.question: Photosynthesis uses light energy to convert CO₂ and water into ________ and oxygen.
questions.explanation: Glucose is the main product of photosynthesis — it stores chemical energy for the plant.

questions.question: ________ is the green pigment in chloroplasts that absorbs light energy.
questions.explanation: Chlorophyll mainly absorbs red and blue light. It reflects green light, which is why leaves appear green.

questions.question: A ________ is a factor that is in short supply and prevents a faster rate of photosynthesis.
questions.explanation: The three main limiting factors are light intensity, CO₂ concentration, and temperature.

questions.question: Plants store glucose as ________, an insoluble form that does not affect osmosis.
questions.explanation: Starch granules are stored in chloroplasts and other plant tissues for use when glucose is needed.

questions.question: Above the optimum temperature, photosynthesis slows because enzymes are ________.
questions.explanation: High temperatures permanently change the shape of enzyme active sites, stopping them from functioning.

questions.question: Increasing ________ concentration increases photosynthesis until another factor becomes limiting.
questions.explanation: CO₂ is a raw material for photosynthesis — more CO₂ means more reactions can occur per unit time.

questions.question: The ________ point is where the rate of photosynthesis equals the rate of respiration.
questions.explanation: At the compensation point, net gas exchange is zero — all O₂ produced is used in respiration.

questions.question: The word equation for photosynthesis is: carbon dioxide + water → ________ + oxygen.
questions.explanation: This is the overall equation: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ (using light energy in chloroplasts).

questions.question: Light ________ follows the inverse square law — doubling distance quarters the intensity.
questions.explanation: Light intensity ∝ 1/d². This means small changes in distance have a large effect on photosynthesis rate.

questions.question: Photosynthesis takes place in the ________.
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and all the machinery needed to carry out photosynthesis.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: converts CO₂ and water into glucose and oxygen using light energy
green pigment that absorbs light energy in chloroplasts
factor in short supply that prevents a faster rate of photosynthesis
insoluble storage form of glucose in plants
light intensity where photosynthesis rate equals respiration rate

cards.definition: the release of energy from glucose using oxygen — produces CO₂ and water as waste products
the release of energy from glucose without oxygen — produces lactic acid in animals or ethanol and CO₂ in yeast
adenosine triphosphate — the energy currency of cells, released when glucose is broken down in respiration
the waste product of anaerobic respiration in animal cells — causes muscle fatigue during intense exercise
the extra oxygen needed after exercise to remove lactic acid and restore normal conditions
anaerobic respiration in microorganisms — yeast converts glucose into ethanol and carbon dioxide
the organelle where aerobic respiration takes place — has a folded inner membrane to increase surface area
the main substrate (fuel) for cellular respiration — broken down to release energy
the first stage of respiration occurring in the cytoplasm — glucose is broken down to pyruvate
the rate at which chemical reactions occur in the body — increases during exercise

questions.question: What are the products of aerobic respiration?
questions.explanation: Aerobic respiration: glucose + oxygen → carbon dioxide + water (+ energy). CO₂ and water are the waste products.

questions.question: What is produced by anaerobic respiration in animal muscle cells?
questions.explanation: In animal cells, anaerobic respiration produces lactic acid. This builds up in muscles and causes fatigue and pain.

questions.question: What is ATP?
options: B) The energy currency of cells
C) A waste product of respiration
questions.explanation: ATP (adenosine triphosphate) is the molecule that transfers energy within cells. It is produced during respiration.

questions.question: What is oxygen debt?
options: A) The oxygen stored in muscles
B) Extra oxygen needed after exercise to remove lactic acid
C) Oxygen used during anaerobic respiration
D) The maximum oxygen uptake during exercise
questions.explanation: Oxygen debt is the extra oxygen needed after intense exercise to oxidise lactic acid, restore glycogen stores, and return the body to its resting state.

questions.question: What does yeast produce during anaerobic respiration?
questions.explanation: Yeast undergoes fermentation — anaerobic respiration that produces ethanol and carbon dioxide from glucose.

questions.question: Where does aerobic respiration mainly take place?
questions.explanation: Aerobic respiration occurs mainly in the mitochondria. The inner mitochondrial membrane is folded to increase surface area for the reactions.

questions.question: Which molecule is the main fuel for respiration?
questions.explanation: Glucose is the primary substrate for cellular respiration. It is broken down to release energy (ATP).

questions.question: What is the word equation for aerobic respiration?
options: A) Glucose + oxygen → carbon dioxide + water
B) Carbon dioxide + water → glucose + oxygen
questions.explanation: Aerobic respiration: glucose + oxygen → carbon dioxide + water. Energy is released and transferred to ATP.

questions.question: Where does glycolysis occur?
questions.explanation: Glycolysis is the first stage of respiration. It occurs in the cytoplasm and splits glucose into pyruvate, producing a small amount of ATP.

questions.question: What happens to metabolic rate during exercise?
questions.explanation: During exercise, cells need more energy. Metabolic rate increases — more glucose and oxygen are used and more CO₂ is produced.

questions.question: Aerobic respiration requires oxygen.
questions.explanation: Aerobic respiration uses oxygen to fully break down glucose, releasing maximum energy as ATP.

questions.question: Anaerobic respiration in animal cells produces ethanol.
questions.explanation: Animal cells produce lactic acid during anaerobic respiration. Ethanol is produced by yeast (fermentation).

questions.question: ATP is the energy currency of cells.
questions.explanation: ATP stores and transfers energy within cells. It is produced during respiration and used to power all cellular processes.

questions.question: Lactic acid is produced during aerobic respiration.
questions.explanation: Lactic acid is produced during anaerobic respiration in animal cells when oxygen is in short supply.

questions.question: Fermentation is carried out by yeast cells.
questions.explanation: Fermentation is anaerobic respiration in yeast — glucose is converted to ethanol and CO₂.

questions.question: Aerobic respiration produces more ATP than anaerobic respiration.
questions.explanation: Aerobic respiration fully breaks down glucose, releasing far more energy (ATP) than anaerobic respiration.

questions.question: Glycolysis occurs in the mitochondria.
questions.explanation: Glycolysis occurs in the cytoplasm — it is the first stage of respiration and does not require mitochondria.

questions.question: Oxygen debt must be repaid after intense exercise.
questions.explanation: After intense exercise, extra oxygen is needed to remove lactic acid by oxidising it in the liver.

questions.question: Glucose is the main substrate for cellular respiration.
questions.explanation: Glucose is the primary fuel for respiration, broken down to release energy stored as ATP.

questions.question: Aerobic respiration takes place in the chloroplasts.
questions.explanation: Aerobic respiration takes place in the mitochondria. Chloroplasts are the site of photosynthesis.

questions.question: Aerobic respiration uses ________ to break down glucose and release energy.
questions.explanation: Aerobic respiration: glucose + oxygen → carbon dioxide + water + energy (ATP).

questions.question: Anaerobic respiration in animals produces ________, which causes muscle fatigue.
questions.explanation: Lactic acid builds up in muscles during intense exercise when oxygen cannot be supplied fast enough.

questions.question: ________ is the energy currency of cells produced during respiration.
questions.explanation: ATP is produced when glucose is broken down. It is used to power all cellular processes.

questions.question: ________ is anaerobic respiration in yeast, producing ethanol and CO₂.
questions.explanation: Fermentation is used in brewing and bread-making as yeast converts glucose to ethanol and CO₂.

questions.question: After intense exercise, ________ debt is repaid by breathing deeply to supply extra oxygen.
questions.explanation: Oxygen debt is the extra oxygen needed to oxidise lactic acid and restore resting conditions.

questions.question: Aerobic respiration mainly occurs in the ________.
questions.explanation: Mitochondria have a folded inner membrane (cristae) to maximise the surface area for aerobic respiration.

questions.question: ________ is the first stage of respiration, occurring in the cytoplasm, splitting glucose into pyruvate.
questions.explanation: Glycolysis produces a small amount of ATP and does not require oxygen.

questions.question: The word equation for aerobic respiration is: glucose + oxygen → ________ + water.
questions.explanation: Carbon dioxide and water are the waste products of aerobic respiration, released via the lungs and skin.

questions.question: ________ is the main fuel (substrate) broken down in cellular respiration.
questions.explanation: Glucose is transported in the blood and enters cells for use in respiration.

questions.question: During exercise, ________ rate increases as cells require more energy.
questions.explanation: Increased metabolic rate means more respiration, more oxygen consumed, and more CO₂ produced.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: releases energy from glucose using oxygen
releases energy from glucose without oxygen
waste product of anaerobic respiration in animal cells
anaerobic respiration in yeast producing ethanol and CO₂
extra oxygen needed after exercise to remove lactic acid

cards.definition: the maintenance of a stable internal environment despite changes in external conditions
the body's rapid communication system — the brain, spinal cord, and nerves coordinate responses to stimuli
a specialised cell that transmits electrical impulses — sensory, relay, or motor
the tiny gap between two neurones — electrical impulses cannot cross directly, so neurotransmitters carry the signal
an automatic, rapid response to a stimulus that does not require conscious thought
the pathway of a reflex: receptor → sensory neurone → relay neurone → motor neurone → effector
a cell or organ that detects a stimulus — e.g. light receptors in the eye, touch receptors in the skin
a muscle or gland that carries out a response — muscles contract; glands secrete hormones
a neurone that carries electrical impulses from receptors to the CNS
a neurone that carries electrical impulses from the CNS to effectors (muscles and glands)

questions.question: What is homeostasis?
options: B) Maintenance of a stable internal environment
C) The process of releasing hormones
questions.explanation: Homeostasis is the regulation of the body's internal environment — keeping conditions such as temperature, pH, and glucose levels constant.

questions.question: What is a synapse?
options: A) A type of receptor cell
B) A gap between two neurones
questions.explanation: A synapse is the tiny gap between two neurones. Neurotransmitter chemicals diffuse across the gap to carry the impulse to the next neurone.

questions.question: What is a reflex action?
options: B) A rapid, automatic response to a stimulus
questions.explanation: Reflexes are rapid, automatic responses that protect the body. They do not involve conscious thought — the response happens before the brain is aware.

questions.question: What is the correct order of a reflex arc?
options: A) Effector → motor neurone → CNS → sensory neurone → receptor
B) Receptor → motor neurone → CNS → sensory neurone → effector
C) Receptor → sensory neurone → CNS → motor neurone → effector
D) CNS → sensory neurone → receptor → motor neurone → effector
questions.explanation: The reflex arc: receptor detects stimulus → sensory neurone → CNS (relay neurone) → motor neurone → effector responds.

questions.question: What type of neurone carries impulses from the CNS to muscles?
questions.explanation: Motor neurones carry nerve impulses from the central nervous system (brain and spinal cord) to effectors (muscles and glands).

questions.question: What is an effector?
options: A) A cell that detects stimuli
B) A cell that carries impulses to the brain
C) A muscle or gland that responds to nerve impulses
questions.explanation: Effectors are the muscles and glands that carry out responses. Muscles contract; glands secrete chemicals such as hormones or enzymes.

questions.question: How do nerve impulses cross a synapse?
options: B) By neurotransmitter chemicals diffusing across the gap
questions.explanation: At a synapse, neurotransmitters are released from one neurone, diffuse across the gap, and bind to receptors on the next neurone.

questions.question: What does a sensory neurone do?
options: A) Carries impulses from the CNS to muscles
B) Connects two neurones in the CNS
C) Carries impulses from receptors to the CNS
questions.explanation: Sensory neurones carry electrical impulses from receptor cells to the central nervous system for processing.

questions.question: Which part of the body is the CNS?
options: D) All nerves in the body
questions.explanation: The central nervous system (CNS) consists of the brain and spinal cord. It processes information and coordinates responses.

questions.question: Why are reflexes important?
options: C) They provide rapid protection without waiting for brain processing
D) They are more accurate than conscious actions
questions.explanation: Reflexes protect the body by producing very fast responses — the signal bypasses conscious processing in the brain.

questions.question: Homeostasis maintains a stable internal environment.
questions.explanation: Homeostasis keeps conditions such as temperature, blood glucose, and water balance within narrow limits.

questions.question: Nerve impulses can jump directly across a synapse without chemicals.
questions.explanation: Nerve impulses cannot cross the synaptic gap electrically — neurotransmitter chemicals diffuse across to carry the signal.

questions.question: A reflex action involves conscious thought.
questions.explanation: Reflexes are automatic and do not require conscious thought — the response occurs before the brain is aware of the stimulus.

questions.question: Motor neurones carry impulses from the CNS to effectors.
questions.explanation: Motor neurones transmit electrical impulses from the central nervous system to muscles and glands (effectors).

questions.question: Sensory neurones carry impulses from the CNS to receptors.
questions.explanation: Sensory neurones carry impulses FROM receptors TO the CNS. Motor neurones carry impulses from the CNS to effectors.

questions.question: The CNS consists of the brain and spinal cord.
questions.explanation: The central nervous system (CNS) processes all incoming information and coordinates appropriate responses.

questions.question: An effector is a cell that detects stimuli.
questions.explanation: An effector is a muscle or gland that carries out a response. Receptors are the cells that detect stimuli.

questions.question: Reflexes protect the body from harm.
questions.explanation: Reflexes such as pulling your hand away from heat or blinking happen automatically to protect the body.

questions.question: Neurotransmitters are released at synapses.
questions.explanation: Neurotransmitters are chemical messengers released from one neurone that diffuse across the synapse to stimulate the next.

questions.question: The reflex arc pathway is: receptor → sensory neurone → relay neurone → motor neurone → effector.
questions.explanation: This is the correct pathway for a simple reflex arc, coordinated by the spinal cord.

questions.question: ________ is the maintenance of a stable internal environment.
questions.explanation: Homeostasis regulates temperature, blood glucose, water balance, and other internal conditions.

questions.question: A ________ is the tiny gap between two neurones where neurotransmitters carry the signal.
questions.explanation: Neurotransmitters diffuse across the synaptic gap and bind to receptors on the next neurone.

questions.question: A ________ action is a rapid, automatic response that does not require conscious thought.
questions.explanation: Reflexes protect the body — they occur faster than conscious reactions because they bypass the brain.

questions.question: ________ neurones carry impulses from the CNS to muscles and glands.
questions.explanation: Motor neurones transmit the 'instruction' from the brain or spinal cord to effectors.

questions.question: ________ neurones carry impulses from receptors to the CNS.
questions.explanation: Sensory neurones carry information about stimuli from receptor cells to the central nervous system.

questions.question: The correct order of the reflex arc is: receptor → sensory neurone → ________ neurone → motor neurone → effector.
questions.explanation: Relay neurones in the spinal cord connect sensory and motor neurones in a reflex arc.

questions.question: Muscles and glands that carry out responses are called ________.
questions.explanation: Effectors respond to motor nerve impulses — muscles contract and glands secrete chemicals.

questions.question: The ________ consists of the brain and spinal cord.
questions.explanation: The central nervous system (CNS) processes sensory information and coordinates responses.

questions.question: Nerve impulses cross synapses via ________ chemicals.
questions.explanation: Neurotransmitters are released from one neurone and bind to receptor proteins on the next.

questions.question: Cells that detect stimuli such as light, heat, or pressure are called ________.
questions.explanation: Receptor cells are specialised to detect specific stimuli and generate nerve impulses.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: maintaining a stable internal environment
gap between neurones where neurotransmitters carry the signal
carries impulses from receptors to the CNS
carries impulses from the CNS to effectors
receptor → sensory → relay → motor → effector

cards.definition: deoxyribonucleic acid — the molecule that carries genetic information in the form of a code
a section of DNA that codes for the production of a specific protein — determines characteristics
a version of a gene — most genes have two or more alleles (e.g. dominant and recessive)
an allele whose characteristic is always expressed, even if only one copy is present
an allele whose characteristic is only expressed when two copies are present
having two identical alleles for a gene — either homozygous dominant (BB) or recessive (bb)
having two different alleles for a gene — e.g. Bb
the genetic make-up of an organism — the alleles it carries for a particular gene
the observable characteristics of an organism — produced by genotype and environment
a grid used to predict the probability of offspring inheriting particular genotypes

questions.question: What does DNA stand for?
questions.explanation: DNA stands for deoxyribonucleic acid — the molecule that stores genetic information in the form of a chemical code.

questions.question: What is a gene?
options: B) A section of DNA coding for a specific protein
C) The entire DNA of an organism
questions.explanation: A gene is a specific section of DNA that codes for a particular protein. Different proteins give different characteristics.

questions.question: What is an allele?
options: B) A version of a gene
C) A type of cell division
D) The full set of an organism's DNA
questions.explanation: An allele is a specific version of a gene. For most genes, there are two or more possible alleles.

questions.question: A dominant allele is expressed when...
options: A) Two copies are present only
B) One or two copies are present
D) The organism is heterozygous only
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed whenever it is present — whether the organism is homozygous dominant (BB) or heterozygous (Bb).

questions.question: What does homozygous mean?
options: A) Having two different alleles for a gene
B) Having one dominant and one recessive allele
C) Having two identical alleles for a gene
D) Having no alleles for a gene
questions.explanation: A homozygous organism has two identical alleles for a gene — either both dominant (BB) or both recessive (bb).

questions.question: What is the genotype of an organism?
options: B) The alleles it carries for a particular gene
C) The number of chromosomes it has
questions.explanation: Genotype refers to the specific alleles an organism carries — its genetic make-up. This determines but does not always equal phenotype.

questions.question: What is phenotype?
options: A) The alleles an organism carries
B) The number of genes an organism has
C) The observable characteristics of an organism
D) The type of cell division used
questions.explanation: Phenotype is what you can observe — height, eye colour, blood type. It is determined by genotype and can also be influenced by environment.

questions.question: What is a Punnett square used for?
options: B) To predict the probability of offspring genotypes
questions.explanation: A Punnett square is a grid that shows all possible combinations of parental alleles in offspring, allowing prediction of genotype and phenotype ratios.

questions.question: If both parents are Bb, what is the probability of a bb offspring?
questions.explanation: A Punnett square cross of Bb × Bb gives: BB, Bb, Bb, bb — a 1:2:1 ratio. bb offspring have a 25% (1 in 4) probability.

questions.question: A recessive characteristic is only expressed when...
options: A) One recessive allele is present
B) Two recessive alleles are present
C) One dominant allele is absent
D) Two dominant alleles are present
questions.explanation: A recessive allele is only expressed in the phenotype when two copies are present (homozygous recessive, bb).

questions.question: DNA stands for deoxyribonucleic acid.
questions.explanation: DNA is the molecule that carries genetic information. It is found in the nucleus of eukaryotic cells.

questions.question: A gene is a section of DNA that codes for a specific protein.
questions.explanation: Genes carry the instructions for making proteins, which determine the characteristics of an organism.

questions.question: All genes have exactly two alleles.
questions.explanation: Most genes have two or more alleles. Some genes have many different alleles in a population.

questions.question: A dominant allele is only expressed when two copies are present.
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed whenever it is present — in both homozygous dominant (BB) and heterozygous (Bb) organisms.

questions.question: Phenotype refers to the alleles an organism carries.
questions.explanation: Phenotype refers to the observable characteristics of an organism. Genotype refers to the alleles it carries.

questions.question: A heterozygous organism has two different alleles for a gene.
questions.explanation: A heterozygous organism (e.g. Bb) has one dominant and one recessive allele for a particular gene.

questions.question: Punnett squares can predict the probability of offspring phenotypes.
questions.explanation: Punnett squares show all possible allele combinations in offspring, allowing prediction of genotype and phenotype ratios.

questions.question: Homozygous dominant and homozygous recessive have the same phenotype.
questions.explanation: Homozygous dominant (BB) and heterozygous (Bb) may show the same phenotype (dominant), but homozygous recessive (bb) shows the recessive phenotype.

questions.question: Environment can influence phenotype.
questions.explanation: While genotype determines potential, environmental factors such as diet, temperature, and sunlight can affect the expressed phenotype.

questions.question: A recessive allele is expressed in a heterozygous organism.
questions.explanation: In a heterozygous organism (Bb), the dominant allele masks the recessive allele. The recessive phenotype is only expressed when the organism is bb.

questions.question: ________ is the molecule in cells that carries genetic information.
questions.explanation: DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) is found in chromosomes in the nucleus and carries the code for all proteins.

questions.question: A ________ is a section of DNA that codes for a specific protein.
questions.explanation: Genes determine characteristics by coding for specific proteins.

questions.question: An ________ is a version of a gene — different alleles produce different characteristics.
questions.explanation: Alleles are alternative forms of a gene found at the same position on homologous chromosomes.

questions.question: A ________ allele is expressed even when only one copy is present.
questions.explanation: Dominant alleles mask the effect of recessive alleles in heterozygous organisms.

questions.question: A ________ allele is only expressed when two copies are present.
questions.explanation: Recessive alleles are masked by dominant alleles — they only show in the phenotype when homozygous recessive.

questions.question: Having two identical alleles for a gene is called ________.
questions.explanation: Homozygous organisms are either BB (homozygous dominant) or bb (homozygous recessive).

questions.question: Having two different alleles for a gene is called ________.
questions.explanation: Heterozygous organisms (Bb) carry one dominant and one recessive allele.

questions.question: The alleles an organism carries for a gene is its ________.
questions.explanation: Genotype is the genetic make-up — the actual alleles present (e.g. BB, Bb, or bb).

questions.question: The observable characteristics of an organism are its ________.
questions.explanation: Phenotype is what you can see or measure — determined by genotype and the environment.

questions.question: A ________ square is used to predict the probability of offspring inheriting certain alleles.
questions.explanation: A Punnett square grid shows all possible combinations from a genetic cross.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: molecule carrying genetic information
section of DNA coding for a specific protein
expressed even when only one copy is present
only expressed when two copies are present
grid used to predict offspring genotype probabilities

cards.definition: the gradual change in inherited characteristics of a population over many generations
the process by which organisms with advantageous traits are more likely to survive and reproduce
the scientist who proposed the theory of evolution by natural selection in 1859
differences in characteristics between individuals of the same species — caused by genes and the environment
a random change in the DNA sequence — the source of new alleles and genetic variation
an environmental factor that affects survival — organisms better adapted to it are more likely to survive
bacteria that survive antibiotic treatment reproduce, passing on resistant genes — an example of natural selection
the formation of new species when populations become reproductively isolated and evolve separately
the permanent disappearance of a species — caused by habitat loss, competition, disease, or climate change
evidence of organisms from the past preserved in rock — supports the theory of evolution

questions.question: What is evolution?
options: A) Change in an individual organism over its lifetime
B) Gradual change in inherited characteristics of a population over generations
C) Adaptation by a single organism to its environment
D) The development of new organs in response to need
questions.explanation: Evolution is the gradual change in the inherited characteristics of a population over many generations, driven by natural selection.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: A) Humans choosing which organisms breed
C) Organisms with advantageous traits surviving and reproducing more
D) All organisms in a population becoming identical
questions.explanation: Natural selection: variation exists → some variants better suited to the environment → they survive and reproduce → advantageous traits passed to offspring.

questions.question: Who proposed the theory of evolution by natural selection?
questions.explanation: Charles Darwin published 'On the Origin of Species' in 1859, proposing that species evolve through natural selection.

questions.question: What causes genetic variation in a population?
questions.explanation: Genetic variation arises from mutations (new alleles) and sexual reproduction (mixing of alleles through meiosis and fertilisation).

questions.question: How does antibiotic resistance in bacteria develop?
options: A) Bacteria choose to become resistant
B) Antibiotics cause bacteria to mutate
C) Resistant bacteria survive and reproduce, passing on resistant genes
D) All bacteria become resistant at the same time
questions.explanation: Antibiotic resistance is natural selection in action — resistant bacteria survive treatment and reproduce, increasing the frequency of resistance genes.

questions.question: What is a mutation?
options: A) A type of cell division
B) A random change in the DNA sequence
C) The removal of a chromosome
D) Inheritance of a recessive allele
questions.explanation: Mutations are random changes to the base sequence of DNA. They create new alleles and are the ultimate source of all genetic variation.

questions.question: What is speciation?
options: A) The extinction of a species
C) Formation of new species from isolated populations
D) Rapid evolution of all individuals
questions.explanation: Speciation occurs when populations become geographically or reproductively isolated and evolve independently until they can no longer interbreed.

questions.question: What type of evidence supports evolution?
options: A) Fossil records, DNA comparison, and anatomical similarities
C) Observation of individual organisms changing
questions.explanation: Multiple lines of evidence support evolution: fossil records, DNA similarities between related species, comparative anatomy, and observed examples of natural selection.

questions.question: What causes extinction?
options: C) Factors such as habitat loss, disease, competition, or climate change
questions.explanation: Extinction occurs when all members of a species die — caused by factors such as habitat destruction, introduced predators, climate change, or disease.

questions.question: What is a selective pressure?
options: A) A type of genetic mutation
B) An environmental factor that affects which organisms survive
C) A medical treatment for bacteria
D) The force of natural selection on a single individual
questions.explanation: A selective pressure is any environmental factor (e.g. predators, disease, climate) that makes some individuals more likely to survive and reproduce than others.

questions.question: Evolution is the change in inherited characteristics of a population over many generations.
questions.explanation: Evolution is the process by which populations change over time through natural selection and other mechanisms.

questions.question: Natural selection acts on individuals, not populations.
questions.explanation: Natural selection acts on populations — individuals cannot evolve, but populations change as better-adapted individuals contribute more offspring.

questions.question: Mutations are always harmful.
questions.explanation: Mutations can be harmful, neutral, or beneficial. Only beneficial mutations are likely to be passed on through natural selection.

questions.question: Antibiotic resistance in bacteria is an example of natural selection.
questions.explanation: Resistant bacteria survive antibiotic treatment and reproduce — passing on the resistance gene. This is natural selection in action.

questions.question: Darwin's theory was immediately accepted by the scientific community.
questions.explanation: Darwin's theory was controversial — it conflicted with religious beliefs and lacked an explanation for how traits were inherited (genetics was not yet understood).

questions.question: Speciation requires populations to become reproductively isolated.
questions.explanation: Speciation occurs when populations cannot interbreed. Isolation allows them to evolve independently until they become different species.

questions.question: The fossil record provides evidence for evolution.
questions.explanation: Fossils show how organisms have changed over time and record the existence of species that are now extinct.

questions.question: All members of a species can become resistant to an antibiotic.
questions.explanation: Only bacteria with the resistance mutation survive. There will always be some bacteria that are susceptible — but overuse of antibiotics increases the proportion that are resistant.

questions.question: Extinction is always caused by human activity.
questions.explanation: Extinction can result from natural processes such as climate change, disease, or competition, as well as human activities like habitat destruction.

questions.question: Variation within a species is necessary for natural selection to occur.
questions.explanation: Natural selection requires variation — if all individuals were identical, there would be nothing to select. Variation is the raw material of evolution.

questions.question: ________ is the gradual change in inherited characteristics of a population over many generations.
questions.explanation: Evolution is driven mainly by natural selection — organisms better suited to their environment survive and reproduce.

questions.question: ________ selection is the process where better-adapted organisms survive and pass on advantageous traits.
questions.explanation: Natural selection requires variation, inheritance, and differential survival and reproduction.

questions.question: ________ are random changes to the DNA sequence — the source of new alleles.
questions.explanation: Mutations create new genetic variants. Most are neutral, some harmful, and a few beneficial.

questions.question: Antibiotic ________ in bacteria is a real-world example of natural selection in action.
questions.explanation: Bacteria with resistance genes survive antibiotic treatment and pass their genes to offspring.

questions.question: The formation of new species from isolated populations is called ________.
questions.explanation: Speciation occurs when populations are isolated long enough to evolve differently and can no longer interbreed.

questions.question: The permanent disappearance of a species is called ________.
questions.explanation: Extinction occurs when all individuals of a species die — it is irreversible.

questions.question: Charles ________ proposed the theory of evolution by natural selection in 1859.
questions.explanation: Darwin's theory was published in 'On the Origin of Species' and revolutionised biology.

questions.question: The ________ record provides evidence of past organisms preserved in rock.
questions.explanation: Fossils show how life has changed over millions of years, supporting evolutionary theory.

questions.question: An environmental factor that affects survival is called a ________ pressure.
questions.explanation: Selective pressures such as predators, disease, and climate drive natural selection.

questions.question: Differences in characteristics between individuals of the same species is called ________.
questions.explanation: Variation is caused by mutations and sexual reproduction — it is essential for natural selection to occur.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: gradual change in inherited characteristics of a population over generations
better-adapted organisms survive and pass on advantageous traits
random change in DNA — source of new alleles
formation of new species from isolated populations
bacteria with resistance genes survive and reproduce — natural selection

cards.definition: all the living organisms in an area and their non-living environment, interacting together
an organism that makes its own food by photosynthesis — e.g. plants and algae. The start of food chains
an organism that gets energy by eating other organisms — primary consumers eat producers
a sequence showing how energy and matter are transferred from one organism to the next through feeding
a network of interconnected food chains showing the feeding relationships in an ecosystem
the total mass of living material at a trophic level — biomass decreases at each level due to respiration, waste, and heat
the position of an organism in a food chain — producers are level 1, primary consumers level 2
organisms (bacteria and fungi) that break down dead organic matter, returning nutrients to the soil
an animal that hunts and kills other animals (prey) for food
organisms competing for the same limited resource — e.g. food, water, space, mates, light

questions.question: What is an ecosystem?
options: A) All living organisms on Earth
B) Living organisms in an area and their non-living environment
C) A food web showing all feeding relationships
D) The soil and climate of a region
questions.explanation: An ecosystem includes all the living organisms (biotic) and non-living factors (abiotic — light, temperature, water) in an area, interacting together.

questions.question: What is a producer?
options: A) An organism that eats plants
B) An organism that makes its own food by photosynthesis
C) A decomposer that breaks down dead matter
questions.explanation: Producers (plants, algae) make their own food through photosynthesis. They form the base of all food chains and webs.

questions.question: Why does biomass decrease along a food chain?
options: A) Organisms at higher trophic levels are smaller
B) Energy is lost as heat, waste, and through respiration at each level
C) Producers store more energy than consumers
D) Decomposers remove biomass from the chain
questions.explanation: At each trophic level, energy is lost through respiration (heat), waste products, and movement — only about 10% is passed to the next level.

questions.question: What is a food web?
options: A) A single feeding sequence from producer to top predator
B) A network of interconnected food chains
C) A diagram showing biomass at each trophic level
D) A classification system for organisms
questions.explanation: A food web shows multiple, interconnected feeding relationships in an ecosystem — more realistic than a single food chain.

questions.question: What do decomposers do?
options: C) Break down dead organic matter and return nutrients to the soil
D) Compete with producers for light
questions.explanation: Decomposers (bacteria and fungi) break down dead organisms and waste, releasing nutrients back into the soil for plants to use.

questions.question: What is competition in ecology?
options: B) Organisms of different species helping each other
C) Organisms competing for the same limited resource
D) The movement of organisms between ecosystems
questions.explanation: Competition occurs when organisms need the same limited resource — interspecific (between species) or intraspecific (within a species).

questions.question: What is the correct term for the feeding position of an organism in a food chain?
questions.explanation: Trophic level describes the position of an organism in a food chain. Producers = level 1; primary consumers = level 2, etc.

questions.question: What is a predator-prey relationship?
options: A) Two species competing for the same food
B) A relationship where one organism kills and eats another
C) Organisms cooperating for mutual benefit
D) Decomposers breaking down dead animals
questions.explanation: In a predator-prey relationship, the predator hunts and eats the prey. Their populations are linked — as prey increases, predator numbers rise; when prey decreases, so do predators.

questions.question: What is biomass?
options: A) The number of organisms at a trophic level
B) The total mass of living material at a trophic level
C) The energy stored in sunlight
D) The amount of carbon dioxide produced
questions.explanation: Biomass is the total dry mass of living material. It decreases at each trophic level because energy is lost between levels.

questions.question: Which organisms are at the base of every food chain?
questions.explanation: All food chains start with producers (plants or algae) that capture energy from sunlight through photosynthesis.

questions.question: An ecosystem includes both living organisms and non-living factors.
questions.explanation: An ecosystem is made up of biotic (living) and abiotic (non-living) components interacting together.

questions.question: Producers get their energy by eating other organisms.
questions.explanation: Producers make their own food through photosynthesis — they do not eat other organisms. Consumers eat other organisms.

questions.question: Biomass increases at each trophic level along a food chain.
questions.explanation: Biomass decreases along a food chain as energy is lost at each level through respiration, heat, and waste.

questions.question: A food web shows only one feeding pathway in an ecosystem.
questions.explanation: A food web shows multiple interconnected food chains, representing the complex feeding relationships in an ecosystem.

questions.question: Decomposers break down dead organic matter and return nutrients to the soil.
questions.explanation: Decomposers (bacteria and fungi) recycle nutrients by breaking down dead organisms and waste products.

questions.question: Competition only occurs between different species.
questions.explanation: Competition can be intraspecific (within the same species) or interspecific (between different species).

questions.question: Energy is lost between trophic levels mainly due to respiration.
