questions.explanation: Most energy is lost at each trophic level through respiration (released as heat), meaning less is available for the next level.

questions.question: All food chains begin with a producer.
questions.explanation: Producers capture energy from sunlight and form the base of all food chains and webs.

questions.question: Predator and prey populations are independent of each other.
questions.explanation: Predator and prey populations are interdependent — changes in one cause changes in the other (cyclical fluctuations).

questions.question: Decomposers are at the top of food chains.
questions.explanation: Decomposers are not usually shown in food chains — they act on organisms at all levels by breaking down dead material.

questions.question: An ________ includes all living organisms and their non-living environment in an area.
questions.explanation: Ecosystems are made up of biotic (living) and abiotic (non-living) components.

questions.question: ________ make their own food by photosynthesis and form the base of all food chains.
questions.explanation: Producers (plants, algae) are the entry point for energy into ecosystems.

questions.question: ________ decreases at each trophic level because energy is lost as heat and waste.
questions.explanation: Only about 10% of biomass is transferred from one trophic level to the next.

questions.question: A ________ web shows the network of feeding relationships in an ecosystem.
questions.explanation: Food webs are more realistic than food chains as they show multiple interconnected relationships.

questions.question: ________ break down dead organic matter and return nutrients to the soil.
questions.explanation: Decomposers (bacteria and fungi) are essential for nutrient cycling in ecosystems.

questions.question: The position of an organism in a food chain is called its ________ level.
questions.explanation: Trophic levels range from producers (level 1) through primary, secondary, and tertiary consumers.

questions.question: ________ occurs when organisms compete for the same limited resource.
questions.explanation: Competition for food, water, space, or mates can limit population sizes.

questions.question: A ________ hunts and kills other animals (its prey) for food.
questions.explanation: Predator-prey relationships cause cyclical fluctuations in both populations.

questions.question: A food ________ shows energy transfer from producer to top predator in a sequence.
questions.explanation: Food chains always start with a producer and end with a top predator.

questions.question: An organism that eats producers is called a ________ consumer.
questions.explanation: Primary consumers (herbivores) eat producers and are eaten by secondary consumers.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: all living organisms and their non-living environment interacting together
organism that makes its own food by photosynthesis
total mass of living material at a trophic level
organism that breaks down dead matter and recycles nutrients
network of interconnected food chains

blocks.block_name: Separate Science: Monoclonal Antibodies & Plant Hormones

cards.definition: identical antibodies from a single clone — highly specific to one antigen
B lymphocyte fused with tumour cell — produces monoclonal antibodies continuously
diagnostic test using monoclonal antibodies to detect specific proteins
monoclonal antibodies carry drugs directly to cancer cells — reducing side effects
a plant hormone — stimulates cell elongation — controls phototropism and gravitropism
growth of plants towards light — controlled by uneven distribution of auxin
growth response to gravity — roots grow towards gravity, shoots away
a plant hormone that stimulates fruit ripening — used commercially to ripen bananas
a plant hormone stimulating seed germination, stem elongation, and fruit development
auxin in rooting powder; ethylene for ripening fruit; gibberellins in brewing

questions.question: What are monoclonal antibodies?
options: B) Identical antibodies from one clone — specific to one antigen
questions.explanation: Hybridoma cells produce large quantities of identical monoclonal antibodies — highly specific to one target antigen.

questions.question: What is a hybridoma cell?
options: A) A type of cancer cell
C) B lymphocyte fused with tumour cell — produces monoclonal antibodies
questions.explanation: Combining antibody-making ability of B lymphocyte with rapid division of tumour cell.

questions.question: How are monoclonal antibodies used in cancer treatment?
options: B) Linked to drugs — target cancer cells specifically
questions.explanation: Targeted drug delivery: antibodies carry drugs/toxins directly to cancer cells — sparing healthy cells.

questions.question: What is auxin?
options: B) A plant hormone stimulating cell elongation — controls tropisms
questions.explanation: Auxin is produced at shoot tips — moves to shaded sides, causing more elongation there → bending towards light.

questions.question: What is phototropism?
options: A) Plants making food from light
B) Plant growth towards light — controlled by uneven auxin distribution
questions.explanation: More auxin accumulates on the shaded side of the shoot → greater cell elongation → shoot bends towards light.

questions.question: What is ethylene used for commercially?
questions.explanation: Fruit is picked unripe then exposed to ethylene gas to ripen — allowing distant transport of fruit.

questions.question: What is gibberellin?
options: A) A plant hormone stimulating seed germination and stem elongation
questions.explanation: Gibberellins promote seed germination, stem elongation, and fruit development — used commercially in brewing (stimulating barley germination) and producing seedless fruit.

questions.question: What is gravitropism?
options: B) Growth response to gravity — roots towards gravity, shoots away
questions.explanation: Auxin redistributes in response to gravity — concentrates on lower side. Roots: inhibited (grow away); shoots: stimulated (grow away from gravity... wait, correct: shoots grow away from gravity = negative gravitropism).

questions.question: What is the ELISA test?
options: C) Detecting specific proteins using monoclonal antibodies — e.g. pregnancy tests
questions.explanation: ELISA: monoclonal antibodies bind to target antigen → colour change indicates presence.

questions.question: How is auxin used commercially?
options: C) As rooting powder — promotes root growth in cuttings
questions.explanation: Auxin-based rooting powder promotes adventitious root growth in plant cuttings — important for horticulture.

questions.question: Monoclonal antibodies are specific to one antigen.
questions.explanation: Each monoclonal antibody binds only to one particular antigen.

questions.question: Hybridoma cells are made by fusing two B lymphocytes.
questions.explanation: B lymphocyte + TUMOUR cell — combining antibody production with rapid division.

questions.question: Auxin causes cell elongation.
questions.explanation: Auxin stimulates cell elongation — more elongation where more auxin concentrates.

questions.question: Phototropism means plants grow away from light.
questions.explanation: Phototropism: shoots grow TOWARDS light — positive phototropism.

questions.question: Ethylene inhibits fruit ripening.
questions.explanation: Ethylene STIMULATES fruit ripening — used commercially to ripen picked fruit.

questions.question: Gibberellins promote seed germination.
questions.explanation: Used in brewing to promote barley germination (malting).

questions.question: Roots show positive gravitropism.
questions.explanation: Roots grow towards gravity — positive gravitropism.

questions.question: Targeted drug delivery using monoclonal antibodies reduces side effects.
questions.explanation: Drugs delivered directly to cancer cells — healthy cells spared.

questions.question: ELISA tests detect specific proteins.
questions.explanation: Highly sensitive diagnostic — pregnancy tests, disease diagnosis.

questions.question: Auxin is produced at the root tip only.
questions.explanation: Auxin is produced at SHOOT tips (and root tips) — redistributes to control growth direction.

questions.question: ________ antibodies are identical — specific to one antigen — from hybridoma cells.
questions.explanation: Used in diagnosis, cancer treatment, and research.

questions.question: ________ is a plant hormone stimulating cell elongation — controls phototropism.
questions.explanation: More auxin on shaded side → more elongation → bending towards light.

questions.question: ________ is plant growth towards light — uneven auxin distribution.
questions.explanation: Positive in shoots (towards light).

questions.question: ________ is plant growth response to gravity — roots towards, shoots away.
questions.explanation: Auxin redistributes under gravity.

questions.question: ________ stimulates fruit ripening — used commercially to ripen bananas.
questions.explanation: Fruit picked unripe then ripened with ethylene gas.

questions.question: ________ promote seed germination and stem elongation.
questions.explanation: Used in brewing and to produce seedless fruit.

questions.question: A ________ cell is B lymphocyte + tumour cell — produces monoclonal antibodies.
questions.explanation: Combines antibody production with immortality.

questions.question: The ________ test detects specific proteins using monoclonal antibodies.
questions.explanation: E.g. pregnancy tests detect hCG hormone.

questions.question: ________ drug delivery uses antibodies to target cancer cells specifically.
questions.explanation: Minimises damage to healthy cells.

questions.question: Auxin is used as ________ powder to promote root growth in cuttings.
questions.explanation: Commercial horticulture application.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: identical — specific to one antigen
cell elongation — controls phototropism and gravitropism
growth towards light — uneven auxin
stimulates fruit ripening
seed germination and stem elongation
